text: I'm interviewing Mr. Lee Roy Muir, Curator of the Baseball Hall of Fame Museum that produced stadium and we're going to talk about Mr. Muir and Baseball on the Eastern Shore. Mr. Muir, a little bit on your background please sir. Okay, I was born in Somerset County in a small village named James Quarter on January 18, 1933 which makes me 71 years old. My parents were Jennings S. and Madeline F. Muir. My sister was named Jane Elizabeth Muir. I completed grade one through 12 and completed several college courses while in the Navy for 20 years. Three children by my first wife, two is a fate who had lost a cancer in 1986. Of a son named Stephenie's 47, Julie's 46, James 43. A second wife, Kathleen had four children and we were married in marks 1990. Her children's names are Cheryl, Michael, Dawn and Kimberly. When you were in the Navy, excuse me, in a row. I know a little bit about that. I guess you would almost assume a lot of people, Mr. Miles, Mr. military, but he saw us sometime in Korea. Okay, sorry they're off to you. All right. Now, to get back to family life, between my wife and I, we have seven children and ten grandchildren. But working construction for our reunion in the Navy in 1953, it's been 20 years in military retiring in 1973. We're thinking of touching that for sure for years and later worked as a sailor for a company in anapolis, Maryland. Okay. I lived in Arnold, Maryland after retirement and moved to Seoul for a in 1989. I became a member of the Eastern Shore Baseball Foundation in 1994 and served on the board of directors and also a secretary of strizer for several years. It is a member of the board of directors until assuming position of curator of the museum in 2004. This has been a rewarding experience as a labor of love for baseball and a people person. My parents moved to Georgetown, Delaware in 1938. I was five and started first grade at you. I had a neighbor friend named Carving the Cates and we were always having a catch with a baseball player. Later we would play sandlife, shoot marbles and play soccer in grade school. All the time during our young teen years we were honing our skills playing baseball. On reaching high school or a thoughts turn some day playing on the high school team. I was a well-tended pitcher with a natural curve and good control. I usually had a 500 percentage of win in loss record and our team would win their share of games each year. After graduating in 1950 at age 17 I played ball for the American Legion post, 22 and some of our games were played on the old Rohobe's Beach Diamond where the Rohobe's Beach Parade played in the beach or tour lake. In 1946, 47, 48 and 49. This was a special treat as well for Gilmur at pitch for Rohobe in 1947. The Bay County Lake was formed and I played for Millsboro in 1950 and 1951. I was scattered by Pittsburgh Parade Scout, Oak Wheeling in early 1951 and was invited to the Parade Training Camp in D.L. and Florida. I was spending two weeks there. I was released and came home to finish with Millboro and the Bay County Lake and we won the championship. I was invited to play on the All-Star team along with my team mates, Demon Mitchell and Bobby Fisher. We played Pokemon Eastern Shore League in a night game. In 1952 I tried out for a Class D team and was sent to Lexington, North Carolina for spring training. This was in the North Carolina League Class D. I signed for to play for $150 per month which wasn't bad for that year. We played all night games and rode an old bus, stayed at a new hotel in Lexington and ate at a boarding house. I didn't have a winning record and was released in July. It was a great experience and I will always look back on it as something special happening in my life. I returned to Delaware and played with Millsboro in the Central Shore League as a first basement where I vetted 267. Millboro won the championship that year against Sharktown. I dare say that there must have been 2,000 fans at that game. We had many great players on the team such as Bob Brody, Part Mitchell, Leroy Jones, Jay Dunnoly, Bobby Knight, Graham Dill, Bobby Fisher, Freddie Edkins, Jesse Millman, Jim Reed and Bill Spencer. Many of these players played for other teams until the Central Shore League folded in 1959. I went into the military in 1953. I had many memorable moments to look back on. During a high school game against Milton Delaware, I picked a player off first base who thought he was hot still. In 1951, I picked him against Snow Hill as truck at 16 batters and that was a special day. I remember playing Chris Beale one very hot day on their home field. They were very poor losers and we beat them 2-1. I'll pitch in a game in the North Carolina State League one night, left handed batter hit a hell-run off of me. And it landed on top of the fence and bounced over. My luck. While playing service ball in California, I pitched at 14 in a game and we lost into 14. Other time while playing in Barcelona, California, one hot in human afternoon, I hit a tower in ball to right field and thought it would be caught by the right field. But he kept rising until it went over the fence for a home run. Another time while playing for the CB team in Davisville, Rio Grande. My catcher was a big man called Big Dan, 6 foot 4, 220 pounds. He would throw the ball back to me harder to me than I could pitch to him. Having seen many baseball games as a young teenager at Old Shive Park in Philadelphia with Ayes and Philly's, I can remember players like Bobby Chance, Rudy Yard, Robin Roberts, Stan Usual. Other ones were Alia Reynolds, Walt Drupal, Neil DeRosher, Frank Mouson, Tal Brickett Jr., Looked Robinson, Jim Palmer, Pete Rose, and the list goes on and on. So many great major league players. They had a complete display of all of the major league women's uniforms, batch balls, pictures, and this sort of thing was really great. But they were good. Yeah. Well, yeah. And if you like some of the girls basketball teams, the girls were real good. And they played with their brothers or the guys and the boys. Sure. Well, some of them just got natural ability. Yeah. And then you know they're going to be an athlete. My dad's from the Midwest. And this sister is a good baseball player because that's what the kid's been out there. They play baseball. Sure. How about a little bit on the old Eastern Shore League? Oh, right. I know. I know there were three phases of the Eastern Shore League. The first feature shore League can be deemed a total success with each team posted an $875 entrance fee and to either build a new part or renovate the existing one, which was one of the things that they had to do in order to get into the league. Yeah. And the six teams chosen to begin a new class D league were Cambridge, Crispield, Hook and Mox City, Salisbury, Laurel Delaware, and Parkesley, Virginia. Parkesley happened to win the very first championship in 1922. And then they won two more after that in 1929 and maybe it was 31 or something like it, one three championships, which for a small town, that's pretty good. Certainly is. Yeah. And of course, this season lasted until 1927 and then in 28 is when they actually voted. Did they blow lack of money? Was it depression or? Several reasons. Financial aid was the big reason because they had set an amount for the players. And of course, a lot of times they couldn't come up with this amount. But tendons started to dwindle. And so they decided to end part of that. They weren't subsidized or have, like we were talking, it's originally working contracts from the major wage. They might buy some of the players that were acting. Oh yeah, but today, the shorebirds are part of the oil and water there. There's money to there. The support. Yeah. They didn't have the support back then like they do today. From the major league. That's true. It's all great and Cambridge would be the only two teams to survive the three distinct phases of the Eastern surely over a span of 28 years. And the only two teams and the reason why the head-der-ups and downs would have possibility of having to pull at times. But it came evident that the downs were overcoming the ups at on July 11, 1928 at the Wycombe Hotel in Tulfbury. The league directors assembled and voted by a margin of 42 to disband the Eastern surely. I'm not going to jump ahead of you if I know. The next one is 1937. The president Roosevelt put out a number of programs in his new deal to help local communities. And I thought maybe some of that new deal money was that filtered in athletics or say like baseball in this area. I'm not sure about that. I mean, the solve bearing there is some things that we talked about. And I still think showing money being within the local areas with murals and things like this, you know, in one of the baseballs that will money in the area. Well, a lot of the people of business men and things, what happened was that when there was one guy impartially who even sold some of his personal possessions in order to keep the team going. Okay, and this is what a lot of the business men would do is they would provide money to keep the team running because of financial situation, which I can understand why. They didn't want to see the baseball go by the wayside. And baseball, what do you say was probably the primary sport in the area at that time. I'm sure we'll get a little bit farther along and journey. Yeah, the sports kind of plan, but it seemed like everybody will slow it to baseball. I tell people when we were growing up, there were three things to do on Sunday. You could go to the beach with your girl, you could go fishing or you could play baseball. And most of the guys chose baseball. Yeah, the day we get ripped out of the video. Do you want to talk a little bit about the span between 28 and 37? Yeah, just a little bit, because that's sort of a downpour, but we also know that's the period in the front end. Well, the teams that played on the shore had some real great players, much talent was here. And a lot of them became major ligters later on. But one of the things that people happen to forget here is the reason why that baseball was revived was because of the famous Cambridge Phillips delicious in the Coca-Cola City Championships here in 1936. Those are local teams. It's two local teams and they had nine professional players who were involved in the local city championship. The attendance of these games sparked the needed interest to revive the second phase of the eagerness really played in 1937. Some of the names responsible for the revitalization were Tom Tibler, who served as president. Later on in other phases, Joe Carr, Art Ellers, who was from the Oklahoma City area, he was in World War I. His career was cut short as a pitcher because he got machine gun in his pitching arm in World War I. But he was one of the ones that sold his personal possessions to keep it going. And of course a lot of these names are John Dutch Brennan and Harry Russell. And all of these men came together. We have one man in Saltbury here by the name of Mr. L. W. Gumbie. There was a prominent businessman here in Saltbury who also had interest in keeping the literature really going. There are Joe's land is known for them baseball. That was quite a day. Yeah. Stand for practice. I know everybody was in a coat fight. Well I tell people they just came from church. Yeah. Okay, what else would you like to cover here? How about the next creation of the 3741 Weekend? One of the things I'd like to point out in regards to 37 is probably the most memorable event to happen here in Saltbury during their time in the Easter tour league. Alright, probably the most memorable moment we were looking back at the 1937 team was. They started the season off and had to park at 21 games because of an ineligible player. Ineligible for a reason. For him, should have not been playing in that league, should have been with another higher league. In other words, they had taken in and brought him in from some of the league and shouldn't have brought him in. So they had certain requirements, certain rules to go by in and a man by the name of Tom Tippler, who said it was right. So he made them park at the 21 games. I think they guessed they won now that 111. You mean if they had them? Yeah, they had them. Well, that was definitely one of the biggest part of them because they ended up winning the championship. They won 48 of their last 58 games. They had a pretty good start, but they overcame that adversity. That's pretty devastating. They were losing 21 games and they went back with 48 of 58. Yeah, that's about it. That's why they won the championship. But if Cooper's town rates the greatest feats in baseball, they have 100 of them. The speed that solves Red Bull off in 37 is rated number 8 out of 100. Jake Flowers, a manager, and he was a Cambridge. He got the manager of the year by the sporting news. And later, he played Major League Baseball and managed and he's inducted here in this hall. To, of course, did you get a chance to see him? I didn't get to see Jake Flowers. I got 37. No, no, I was only 4 years old. Yeah. Anyhow, there was five of those players on that team that eventually went to Washington Senators and played Major League Baseball. They probably had a contract with the Senators. They did. They were affiliated with the Senators at the time. So, the whole world won the reason that the league was able to team the off-rate was because they got money for some board eyes. And, of course, they could get players too. But, yeah, they're intermesting. And the grand baseball must have been pretty good. I would say it was actually. I have an uncle who coached and managed in the Eastern Shore League and played a lot of baseball. And I've heard him say many times that if he could pick a group of ball players from this area, the Del Morro Peninsula, that he could play against any double-a or triple-a team, he said that to confidence that he had in the players. And, of course, a lot of players from the Eastern Shore that they were happy just to stay at home that didn't want to move. You know, geographically, they liked it here. And even though they were offered to sign contracts with those spring trains, they wouldn't do it. That's that sand between their toes. But anyhow, in 1941, of course, you know, with the war on, there was really two main reasons why the league pulled. And one of them was that two of the teams dropped out of Pokemon City. And like it was, I'm not forgetful, but anyhow, two of them dropped out. And they showed only two teams showed a profit. And one of them was Sal's break and had it not been for World War II. They think that the Eastern Shore League would have kept survive for a few more years. But I'm just understandable. I mean, when teams dropped out, it's assigned that something wrong. Well, of course, I suspect with World War II starting, all of a sudden there was a tremendous patriotism for a river. And a lot of the guys who were playing ball were into the military. So you sort of had, and with the towns being relatively small, and you take a group of men or drafted into the war, that's going to take away from the team's strength. And so if they don't have the strength to compete, they're not going to play. So understandable. So the war took a lot of people away. That was kind of your formative years of growing up. That's what I was saying. When you were playing, I guess they still had the sports around, but it was a little bit different. It was? Did you have pretty good following for the high school sports that you mentioned? I mean, you had there in the war years? What you had to remember, I didn't play during the war years, I'm 41 through 45, because I was still at that age group where you couldn't play organized baseball. We played sand, lot, and you know, catch, and you know, this sort of thing. But then when the war was over, that's when things really got good again. People were becoming of age, you guys were coming back from the war, and they wanted to do something. And baseball was that was it. There were several people that were individuals that were responsible for surviving each or surely in 1946. And Mr. John Curry of centerville was one of Mr. WK Nodz of Federal Bird. Dr. Walter Greer of Nilford, who was personal friends with Connie Mack of the athletics, and would supply him occasionally with players from the Eastern tour here. And Fred Lucas of Cambridge, who was magnificent, individual, and was probably the most influential person in the redevelopment of the league, with his knowledge of the ways of how the short people felt and acted during his nine years he had lived in the Cambridge area. And the Cambridge Canter in 1922 to 1927 and the Cambridge Cardinals, a lot of people don't remember that. The Cambridge team was a cardinal at one time, and that was in 37 to 41. And along with the solid-bury Indians, far and away the most successful franchises of the two previous each of your leagues. So Mr. Lucas knew that if on major league clubs would provide financial assistance in either renovating old parts or building new parts, the others would follow the leader in their support. So, and he thought, well, if this happened, then maybe the league might presume again. So, what he did was he went to Brooklyn on a several occasions and joined the one or a nineteen forty-five to drive a bargain with Branch Rickey when the organization of the each of your league. And specifically to have the Dodgers support a team in Cambridge. I wanted, I did saw the Cambridge Canter names and their manners, I saw pictures on the wall here, it said Dodgers. He did this, he must have been really a very personal individual because what he did was he invited Branch Rickey down here on the Eastern School for some fishing trips and then later for some ducking and hunting trips. And this seemed to work well as Mr. Rickey agreed to build a park in Cambridge, $60,000, as long as they would call it the Cambridge Dodgers. And so that's exactly what happened with it. And it's how things got started again. And so that's how things got started.
